does: This paper introduces a gradient-free pruning framework that identifies and removes parameters responsible for unsafe behaviors in large language models while preserving model utility. The method uses an attribution mechanism to target unsafe subnetworks without requiring expensive gradient computations, making it resource-efficient and applicable to various architectures and quantized variants. Empirical evaluation demonstrates substantial reductions in unsafe generations, improved robustness against jailbreak attacks, and minimal performance degradation across tested models like Mistral and LLaVA.
connects: This work builds on the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis, which proposes that neural networks contain sparse subnetworks capable of achieving full performance. It extends alignment research beyond existing methods like supervised fine-tuning and RLHF, which focus on encouraging preferred outputs but do not explicitly eliminate unsafe subnetworks. The paper also connects to mechanistic interpretability and safety literature that seeks to understand and modify specific model behaviors, addressing the gap where even aligned models retain unsafe capabilities learned during pre-training.
enables: This framework opens avenues for post-hoc safety improvements that complement rather than replace existing alignment methods, potentially making safety interventions more efficient and scalable. It suggests future work on understanding the structure of unsafe representations in language models and whether similar pruning approaches could target other undesirable behaviors beyond safety. The gradient-free attribution method could inspire broader applications in selective model surgery, while the safety-ticket perspective may lead to new theoretical understanding of how harmful and beneficial capabilities are distributed in neural networks.
